item.title: Reducing NPLs requires more than directives. [ newagebd ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, GPT-OSS-120B, GPT-OSS-20

IT IS welcome that the Bangladesh  Bank has issued specific instructions to 29  banks struggling with high levels of non-performing loans. Among these 29 banks, a few are burdened with NPL ratios exceeding 80–90 per cent. A number of state-owned banks are also grappling with defaulted loans ranging from 20 to 60 per cent. In separate meetings with the managing...

item.title: The republic of leaks. [ newagebd ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B

I GREW up in a small thana sohor, where everyone knew everyone else’s business long before the official record did. A government officer bought a new motorcycle, and within an hour the bazaar had already decided whether he had stolen the money. A school teacher received a promotion and someone inevitably whispered that he must have ‘managed’ it. We learned...

item.title: When the rain, the runoff, and the tide arrive together. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, GPT-OSS-120B

On the night of July 4, 2025, the Guadalupe River in Texas Hill Country rose about 26 feet in 45 minutes. Camp Mystic, a girls' summer camp on its banks, lost 27 campers, counselors, and its director before dawn. More than 135 people died across the region in that single flood, according to state investigative findings. What made the event so lethal was not simply how much rain fell. The National Weather Service's own warnings that night cited rainfall rates climbing to several inches an hour, a pace that turned a river into a wall of water before most people even understood a flood was underway. This is the point that gets lost in most conversations about flooding, in Texas and in Bangladesh alike. The number that matters most in the changing climate is often not the total rainfall for the day, but how fast it arrives. A given volume of rain spread over 12 hours is manageable. The same volume in 90 minutes can be fatal. Chittagong found this out on its own terms this July, when the city recorded about 410 millimetres of rain in 24 hours, according to a Dhaka Tribune report, the heaviest single-day total the port city had seen in 42 years. The rain fell hardest while the Karnaphuli River was at high tide, and while runoff from the surrounding hills poured into a drainage network already at its limit. A city I did not expect to see this in I grew up in Comilla, and waterlogging is not something I remember from my childhood there. A hard rain meant wet streets that dried out again by the next day. That is no longer true. Comilla now floods most summers, sometimes badly enough that students have had to reach their exam centres by boat. Comilla has no hills to cut and no tide to fight. What it has, like dozens of other urbanizing cities across Bangladesh and beyond, is a much less dramatic but equally potent sequence: Canal margins and low channels lost to construction, drains left uncleared for years, and a habit, shared by households, markets, and construction sites alike, of treating the nearest drain as the easiest place to get rid of waste. None of that needed a hill or a coastline. It only needed disregard for the environment and indifferent authorities. Two cities, two different problems Dhaka and Chittagong show the same underlying pattern at a larger, more studied scale, each with its own mechanism layered on top. Dhaka's story is mostly about disappearing land. According to a study on canal encroachment in Dhaka, the city has lost roughly 60% of its wetlands and 65% of its canals over the past 30 years, paved over as construction spread. Retention ponds that once held monsoon runoff have shrunk sharply in the areas where records exist. That is a loss of natural capacity to absorb water, separate from anything happening in the pipes. The piped network is its own weak point. A Daily Star investigation found that Dhaka's main drainage authority maintains roughly 370 kilometres of storm drainage pipe, covering only about a fifth of the city's 400 square kilometre area, with seven different agencies each looking after their own section and little coordination between them. So even where canals are intact, the underground system meant to feed them is often undersized, aging, or simply absent. Chittagong has, or used to have, a hilly terrain and sits next to a coastline, a perfect combination for compound flooding where bidirectional flow coming from the sea and the land overwhelms the natural and built drainage infrastructure. Chittagong has also historically seen heavier monsoon rain because of its proximity to the sea. When a hillside is cut for construction, it destroys a delicate hydrologic balance. As in Dhaka, loss of natural drainage to land grabbing compounds the strain on what remains of the natural drainage pattern. According to a 2023 Daily Star report, the city's own drainage authority now counts 56 canals where 72 once existed, and green space has fallen below 15% of the city's land. Underground drainage fares no better. A recent engineering study found that large parts of the city have no properly connected underground network at all, which means canal restoration alone will not solve the problem even where it succeeds. Underneath both cities' specific drivers of waterlogging sits a simpler failure that needs no special geography to operate: Neglect. Drains and canals that are not regularly cleared silt up regardless of how well they were designed, and clearing them depends on maintenance schedules and budgets that routinely lose out to flashier priorities. An ordinary afternoon shower is often enough to expose a canal that has not been desilted in years, or a drain choked with waste that residents and businesses put there themselves. This pattern is not confined to Dhaka and Chittagong, or even to Bangladesh. Wetlands, floodplains, and canal margins disappear first precisely because they look empty between storms. Each time this happens, in any city following the same sequence, the next flood has less capacity to work with than the one before it. Plans that stop keeping up Dhaka's current drainage plan, drafted by WASA in 2015, assumed 40% of rainfall would soak naturally into the ground, and its pumping stations were sized to match. That was a reasonable estimate at the time, according to an urban planner quoted in a July 2026 Daily Star report. As concrete replaced open soil across the city in the years since, actual infiltration fell well below that number, and the plan was never revised. The same report notes that the city's pumps can now handle only about 40% of the current load. Chittagong's plan is older and carries a different kind of stale assumption. Its Storm Water Drainage and Flood Control Master Plan was drafted between 1993 and 1995. One specific flaw stands out: According to a paper in the Journal of Bangladesh Institute of Planners, drain widths were fixed at 2 to 3 feet regardless of whether the road above them was 20 feet or 60 feet wide, so as development scaled up around them, the drains never did. Hill cutting, meanwhile, is banned under a separate national law, the Environment Conservation Act of 1995, but enforcement has been too weak to stop it. Spending has not been the constraint Chittagong's experience argues against the idea that money is the missing piece. According to a recent statement from the finance minister, four major flood-mitigation projects have been running for close to a decade, with roughly Tk 14,400 crore allocated and about Tk 10,400 crore of it already spent. A separate Daily Star report found that the largest single project, covering 36 canals, has seen its cost rise from about Tk 5,600 crore to roughly Tk 8,600 crore, with its deadline pushed from 2020 to 2026. Over that same period, according to the same report, the city has lost 47 of its original 104 canals to encroachment and sedimentation. Money spent building fixed infrastructure to an unrevised design, without enforcement, coordination, or updated assumptions behind it, buys less every year the city keeps changing. A bigger budget applied to the same static approach does not fix that problem. It just makes it more expensive. What this argues for None of this argues against having a master plan. It argues against treating one as a document written once and left alone for 20 years. A better version is a living plan: Built and updated at the scale of individual drainage basins, with lessons from each basin carried into the next. In practice, that means: Jasim Imran is Professor in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering at the University of South Carolina. The opinions expressed here are the author's own. Figures cited in this piece were drawn from publicly available sources.

item.title: Spray and pray is not an export strategy. [ dawn ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, GPT-OSS-120B

Somewhere in Sialkot, a surgical instruments manufacturer is holding a purchase order from a distributor in Frankfurt.

Someone in Washington, DC, is still trying to figure out how to import mangoes from Pakistan without going through the hassle of asking relatives or going through an informal distribution chain, while some kid in Oslo wants to buy a Trionda (the official football of the FIFA World Cup 2026), but just doesn’t know where to get it officially.

The demand in these cases is real, the margins work, and the buyers are willing to pay a premium. The supplier is willing to ship directly, but it doesn’t know how to do the same, and because of this information asymmetry, its bank declines because it isn’t aware of who the buyer is, the working capital cycle does not fit any product on the shelf, and a multitude of other reasons.

Summer transitions to Fall, the orders detailed above lapse. Nothing in the national accounts records this event, yet it is precisely where Pakistan’s export stagnation lives.

Firms are 80pc more likely to qualify for support on traditional products than on new ones

The instinctive policy response to weak exports has always been to throw more money at the problem, but as the Notorious B.I.G once wrote, the more money we come across, the more problems we see. Another refinance line, another subsidy window, or another mark-up support scheme is the reflex response — there is no shortage of loanable funds, but there is a dearth of loanable funds for small and medium enterprises (SMEs), which is compounded by a shortage of structures that convert commercially viable transactions into bankable ones.

It is estimated that the Export Finance Scheme reaches roughly five per cent of exporters, whereas long-term financing under the Long- Term Financing Facility (LT FF) reaches fewer than 1pc, in terms of number of exporters. The top 100 exporters, who account for about 40pc of export value, absorb twothirds of all export finance, and 86pc of LT FF flows into textiles and allied segments.

As per Gonzalo (2024) in “Evaluating the impact of export finance support on firm-level export performance: evidence from Pakistan”, the evaluated impact is real but narrow, as participants grow export volume by seven to 11 percentage points, with no statistically significant effect on the number of products exported or markets served.

Firms are 80pc more likely to qualify for support on traditional products than on new ones. In effect, we finance more of what we already sell, to buyers we already know. Such schemes only finance scale, but do not finance discovery.

The structural arithmetic shows what this has cost us. The average Pakistani exporter ships $1.4 million a year, one-third of Bangladesh’s $3.8m. The median exporter ships just $68,000. Only a third of our smallest exporters move up a size class over five years, against 45pc in Bangladesh and 53pc in China.

Ten products dominate the merchandise basket, and it has barely diversified in two decades. This concentration has survived every exchange rate cycle; it is not a cyclical artefact, but the embedded outcome of how we allocate credit, which is through a spray and pray approach.

The correction, therefore, is not to spread money thinner across the same basket, but to point risk capital deliberately at the low base, ie the HS [Harmonised System] lines where Pakistan’s export value is small relative to demonstrated capability and global demand, and where the marginal rupee of finance changes the trajectory rather than the volume.

The targeting logic is simple to follow, ie rank the tariff lines, strip out the mature chapters that already absorb concessional finance, and identify the top 100 low-base HS codes where the constraint is financeable.

The next step is to discover those firms, and within those segments sit roughly the top 2,000 SMEs, the firms already producing, already shipping something, but stuck below the size class where banks take them seriously.

Growth in exports will not come from financing the same textile mill a hundredth time with concessional capital, but from the two-thousandth SME in the ninety-ninth HS code.

Although simply settling refunds of exporters operating at scale is also a fruit that is not just low-hanging, but actually on the ground.

Each firm can then be matched to one of three discovery pathways which can be a new geography, where an established product enters a market Pakistan barely serves; it can be a new buyer, where a firm graduates from intermediaries to direct counterparties; or a new product, where a firm scales a line from a low base.

Market entry needs 180- to 360-day facilities and certification finance, wherein buyer acquisition needs transaction-matched cover against counterparty risk, while product discovery needs 90- to 270-day working capital plus long-tenor upgrade finance. One refinance rate cannot price all three, but tailored structures can solve these problems and unlock discovery pathways.

This is where credit or trade guarantees unlock some of the value. A discovery-stage transaction fails when any one of five requirements is missing, which can be certification, a firm order, financier-grade documentation, working capital, and the plant upgrade that delivers specification.

These constraints are sequential, as the certification gates the order, the order gates the cash flow, and the cash flow gates the finance.

A partial credit guarantee, wrapped around a properly structured transaction, changes the risk calculus without replacing its judgement.

The lender continues to originate, price, and retain customers, but is more comfortable with a structured approach driven by policy and more directed towards firms actually scaling up, rather than hoping to find diamonds in the rough.

Pakistan’s food portfolio shows the method in granular detail. Pakistan’s top 50 non-rice food products earn $2.94 billion today and could quintuple to $14.7bn over five years. The binding constraints are nameable per HS code, such as quality segregation for maize, cleaning and grading for sesame, sanitary and phytosanitary and approved plants for beef, cold chain for onions and mangoes.

A granular review of data clearly suggests that processed lines already earn 31pc more per kilogram than raw ones ($0.701 against $0.535). None of these is a subsidy problem, but these are price discovery problems that can be solved through targeted capital expenditure, or working capital facilities, rather than vanilla facilities.

The decision in front of policymakers is not whether to spend more on exports. It is whether the next rupee deepens the top 100 exporters, or makes the next 2,000 firms in the next 100 product lines bankable, and scalable enough to quintuple their exports in five years. We already finance the volume, but discovery requires a structure and a thought process that does not belong to the late nineteen hundreds.

The writer is an assistant professor of practice at IBA and CEO of National Credit Guarantee Company Limited.

Published in Dawn, The Business and Finance Weekly, July 20th, 2026
